doc.summary: The World Investment Report 2020, a 30th-anniversary edition by UNCTAD, forecasts a dramatic fall in global Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) by up to 40% in 2020 due to the COVID-19 pandemic, dropping below $1 trillion for the first time since 2005. This crisis, coupled with the new industrial revolution, economic nationalism, and sustainability trends, is expected to transform international production by 2030, leading to shorter value chains and declining investment in physical assets. Developing economies are projected to be hit hardest. The report also highlights insufficient private sector investment in Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) since 2015 and proposes an Action Plan for Investment in the SDGs, emphasizing the need for a shift in development strategies and increased cooperation.
key_points: Global Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) flows are projected to decrease by up to 40% in 2020 due to the COVID-19 pandemic, falling below $1 trillion for the first time since 2005.
FDI is expected to decrease by a further 5 to 10% in 2021, with a recovery potentially starting in 2022.
The COVID-19 crisis, new industrial revolution, economic nationalism, and sustainability trends are catalysts for a structural transformation of international production by 2030.
Developing economies are anticipated to experience the largest fall in FDI due to reliance on GVC-intensive and extractive industries and limited economic support measures.
International private sector flows to four out of ten key Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) areas have not substantially increased since their adoption in 2015.
The report presents an Action Plan for Investment in the SDGs to translate increased interest in SDG finance into actual investments, especially in least developed countries.
The overall trend in international production points towards shorter value chains, higher concentration of value added, and declining international investment in physical productive assets.
More than 70 countries have implemented investment policy measures to mitigate negative FDI effects or protect domestic industries from foreign takeovers during the pandemic.

# World Investment Report 2020

## Summary

The World Investment Report 2020, a 30th-anniversary edition by UNCTAD, forecasts a dramatic fall in global Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) by up to 40% in 2020 due to the COVID-19 pandemic, dropping below $1 trillion for the first time since 2005. This crisis, coupled with the new industrial revolution, economic nationalism, and sustainability trends, is expected to transform international production by 2030, leading to shorter value chains and declining investment in physical assets. Developing economies are projected to be hit hardest. The report also highlights insufficient private sector investment in Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) since 2015 and proposes an Action Plan for Investment in the SDGs, emphasizing the need for a shift in development strategies and increased cooperation.

## Key Points

- Global Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) flows are projected to decrease by up to 40% in 2020 due to the COVID-19 pandemic, falling below $1 trillion for the first time since 2005.
- FDI is expected to decrease by a further 5 to 10% in 2021, with a recovery potentially starting in 2022.
- The COVID-19 crisis, new industrial revolution, economic nationalism, and sustainability trends are catalysts for a structural transformation of international production by 2030.
- Developing economies are anticipated to experience the largest fall in FDI due to reliance on GVC-intensive and extractive industries and limited economic support measures.
- International private sector flows to four out of ten key Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) areas have not substantially increased since their adoption in 2015.
- The report presents an Action Plan for Investment in the SDGs to translate increased interest in SDG finance into actual investments, especially in least developed countries.
- The overall trend in international production points towards shorter value chains, higher concentration of value added, and declining international investment in physical productive assets.
- More than 70 countries have implemented investment policy measures to mitigate negative FDI effects or protect domestic industries from foreign takeovers during the pandemic.
